docs.text:
• [[README]] -- API endpoint reference index
  • [[agent-executions]] -- Track agent execution history
  • [[agent-schedules]] -- Schedule periodic agent runs
  • [[authentication]] -- Authentication methods and token management
  • Faraday Agent Dispatcher documentation

docs.title: Faraday Authentication & API Tokens — Complete Guide
docs.text:
Faraday Server v5.10.0+ (Community & Commercial Editions)

This guide covers every aspect of authenticating with the Faraday Security Platform: session management, API token lifecycle, agent tokens, SSO/LDAP integration, and security best practices. For the raw endpoint reference, see [[authentication-endpoints]].

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Authentication Methods Overview
  2. Session-Based Authentication
  3. API Token Authentication
  4. Basic Authentication
  5. Agent Tokens
  6. WebSocket Authentication
  7. User Roles & Permissions
  8. Token Configuration & Expiration
  9. Token Revocation
  10. SSO & LDAP Integration
  11. CSRF Protection
  12. Security Best Practices
  13. Troubleshooting

docs.title: Authentication Methods Overview
docs.text:
Faraday supports four authentication mechanisms, parsed from the Authorization HTTP header in app.py:load_user_from_request:

Method Header Format Use Case CSRF Required Session Cookie Cookie: session=... Web UI, interactive scripts Yes (for writes) API Token (JWT) Authorization: Token <jwt> Automation, CI/CD, scripts No Basic Auth Authorization: Basic <base64> Quick one-off requests No Agent Token Authorization: Agent <token> Faraday Agent Dispatcher No

All API endpoints require authentication by default. The default_login_required before-request handler in app.py rejects anonymous requests with HTTP 401 unless the endpoint is explicitly marked is_public = True.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Client sends credentials to POST /_api/login
  2. Server validates credentials via Flask-Security
  3. Server creates a server-side session and returns an HTTP-only session cookie
  4. Client includes the cookie in all subsequent requests
  5. For state-changing requests (POST, PUT, DELETE), a CSRF token is also required

docs.title: Login
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -c cookies.txt \
  -d '{"email": "faraday@example.com", "password": "changeme"}'

Success (200): 

{
  "response": {
    "user": {
      "authentication_token": null
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Session Verification
docs.text:
Verify that a session is still valid and retrieve the CSRF token:

curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/session \
  -b cookies.txt

Response: 

{
  "csrf_token": "IjJhNmY4ZDk3ZTBk...",
  "preferences": { ... },
  "permissions": { ... },
  "user_id": 1
}

The response includes:

Field Type Description csrf_token string CSRF token for state-changing requests preferences object User's stored preferences permissions object Permission map (currently all-allowed) user_id integer Database ID of the authenticated user

docs.title: Idle Session Timeout
docs.text:
Sessions expire after a configurable period of inactivity. Configure in ~/.faraday/config/server.ini:

[faraday_server]
idle_session_timeout = 30

Where the value is in minutes. Only server-hitting activity (clicking buttons, submitting forms, navigating pages) resets the timer. Simply viewing a page without interaction does not.

docs.title: Logout
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/logout \
  -b cookies.txt

After logout the session cookie is invalidated server-side. Discard the cookie file.

docs.title: API Token Authentication
docs.text: API tokens are the recommended method for automation, scripts, CI/CD pipelines, and any non-interactive use. They are stateless JWT tokens that do not require cookie management or CSRF tokens.

docs.title: Token Architecture
docs.text:
Faraday API tokens are JSON Web Tokens (JWT) signed with HS512 (HMAC-SHA512). The JWT payload contains:

Claim Description user_id User's fs_uniquifier (UUID from Flask-Security) validation_check Hash of the user's current password iat Issued-at timestamp (Unix epoch) exp Expiration timestamp (Unix epoch)

The signing key is the Flask application's SECRET_KEY.

Key design property: The validation_check claim contains a hash of the user's password at token-issuance time. If the user changes their password, the hash no longer matches and all previously issued tokens are immediately invalidated — without requiring a token blacklist or revocation list.

docs.title: Generating a Token
docs.text:
Tokens are obtained via GET /_api/v3/token. This endpoint requires an existing authenticated session (cookie or Basic auth):

# Step 1: Login
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -c cookies.txt \
  -d '{"email": "faraday@example.com", "password": "changeme"}'

# Step 2: Get token
TOKEN=$(curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/token \
  -b cookies.txt)

echo "$TOKEN"

Response (plain text — the raw JWT string): 

eyJhbGciOiJIUzUxMiIsImlhdCI6MTcwOTAzMTU3OCwiZXhwIjoxNzA5MDc0Nzc4fQ...

Alternatively, use Basic auth to skip the login step:

TOKEN=$(curl -k -s --user "faraday@example.com:changeme" \
  https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/token)

Audit trail: Every token request is logged to the audit log with the username, source IP, and timestamp.

docs.title: Using the Token
docs.text:
Include the token in the Authorization header with the Token prefix:

# List workspaces
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN"

# Create a vulnerability (no CSRF needed with token auth)
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest/vulns/ \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "name": "SQL Injection",
    "severity": "critical",
    "parent": 42,
    "parent_type": "Host",
    "type": "Vulnerability"
  }'

docs.title: Python Example
docs.text:
import requests

BASE = "https://localhost:5985"
session = requests.Session()
session.verify = False  # For self-signed certs

# Obtain token via Basic auth
resp = session.get(
    f"{BASE}/_api/v3/token",
    auth=("faraday@example.com", "changeme")
)
token = resp.text.strip('"')

# Use token for all subsequent requests
headers = {"Authorization": f"Token {token}"}
workspaces = session.get(f"{BASE}/_api/v3/ws/", headers=headers)
print(workspaces.json())

docs.title: Token Scoping & Permissions
docs.text:
Current state (v5.10.0): API tokens carry the full permissions of the authenticated user. There is no per-token scope restriction. A token issued to an admin user grants admin-level access to all API endpoints.

Persistent tokens (commercial feature): The database includes a UserToken model with a scope field supporting four values:

Scope Purpose gitlab GitLab integration tokens scheduler Scheduled task tokens service_desk Service desk integration jira Jira integration tokens

These scoped tokens also have alias (human-readable name), expires_at (optional expiration date), and revoked (boolean) fields. They are used by commercial-edition integrations but are not exposed through the community API.

docs.title: Basic Authentication
docs.text:
Faraday supports HTTP Basic authentication for convenience. Credentials are sent base64-encoded in the Authorization header:

curl -k -s --user "faraday@example.com:changeme" \
  https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/

This is equivalent to:

curl -k -s -H "Authorization: Basic $(echo -n 'faraday@example.com:changeme' | base64)" \
  https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/

Caution: Basic auth sends credentials with every request. Prefer API tokens for production automation. Basic auth is best for quick one-off requests or initial token retrieval.

docs.title: Agent Tokens
docs.text: Agent tokens are a separate authentication mechanism used exclusively by the Faraday Agent Dispatcher for registering and communicating with the server.

docs.title: Agent Registration Token (TOTP)
docs.text:
The agent registration token is a time-based one-time password (TOTP) generated using pyotp. It is used during the initial agent registration handshake.

Endpoint: GET /_api/v3/agent_token

curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/agent_token \
  -b cookies.txt

Response: 

{
  "token": "123456",
  "expires_in": 45,
  "total_duration": 60
}

Field Description token Current TOTP code expires_in Seconds until this code expires total_duration Total TOTP interval (default: 60 seconds)

Configuration:

Setting Default Description agent_registration_secret Auto-generated TOTP secret key agent_token_expiration 60 (seconds) TOTP interval duration

The agent_registration_secret is automatically generated on first server start and stored in ~/.faraday/config/server.ini.

docs.title: Persistent Agent Token
docs.text:
Once an agent is registered, it receives a persistent 64-character random token stored in the agent database table. This token is used for ongoing agent-to-server communication:

Authorization: Agent <64-char-token>

Agent tokens are validated by direct database lookup (not JWT). If the agent token is not found, the server returns 403 Forbidden.

docs.title: Agent WebSocket Token
docs.text:
For real-time communication, agents obtain short-lived WebSocket tokens:

curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/agent_websocket_token \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"token": "<agent-persistent-token>"}'

The returned token is signed with itsdangerous.TimestampSigner using a websocket_agent salt and has a maximum age of 60 seconds.

docs.title: WebSocket Authentication
docs.text: Faraday uses WebSocket connections for real-time updates (new vulnerabilities, host changes, command output, etc.). WebSocket tokens are workspace-scoped.

docs.title: Obtaining a WebSocket Token
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X GET \
  https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/my_workspace/websocket_token \
  -b cookies.txt

Response: 

{
  "token": "d3Nfd29ya3NwYWNlX3Rva2VuX2V4YW1wbGU..."
}

docs.title: Connecting to WebSocket
docs.text:
wss://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/my_workspace/websocket?token=<token>

User WebSocket tokens are signed with itsdangerous.TimestampSigner using a websocket salt. The token encodes the workspace ID and is validated on connection.

Requirements: - The user must have at least read access to the target workspace - The token is short-lived; obtain a fresh token before each connection

docs.title: User Roles & Permissions
docs.text:
Faraday has four built-in roles:

Role Description admin Full system access, user management pentester Standard penetration tester access asset_owner Asset/infrastructure owner view client Read-only client reporting access

Current state (v5.10.0): The permission system in api/base.py currently returns "allowed" for all permissions for all authenticated users. The RBAC infrastructure is in place (roles, permission checks) but enforcement is handled at the application layer rather than per-endpoint. All authenticated users effectively have the same API access level regardless of role.

Role assignments affect the web UI experience (menu items, available actions) more than API-level access control.

docs.title: API Token Expiration
docs.text:
The API token lifetime is configured via api_token_expiration in ~/.faraday/config/server.ini:

[faraday_server]
api_token_expiration = 43200

The value is in seconds. The effective default depends on your deployment:

Deployment Method Default Value Duration Docker 604800 7 days Bare-metal (server.ini) 43200 12 hours Python fallback (no config) 86400 24 hours

Important: These three different defaults can cause confusion. Check your actual server.ini to confirm which value is in effect.

docs.title: Renewing Expired Tokens
docs.text:
Tokens cannot be refreshed. When a token expires, the client must:

  1. Re-authenticate (login or Basic auth)
  2. Request a new token from GET /_api/v3/token

docs.title: Password-Based Invalidation
docs.text:
Changing a user's password immediately invalidates all outstanding tokens for that user. This happens because the JWT contains a validation_check claim with a hash of the password at issuance time. On each request, the server re-hashes the current password and compares it to the claim. If they differ, the token is rejected with 401.

This is a security feature: if a token is compromised, changing the password revokes all tokens without needing a token blacklist.

docs.title: Automatic Revocation
docs.text: Trigger What Gets Revoked Mechanism Password change All JWT tokens for that user Password hash mismatch in JWT validation_check claim Token expiration The expired token JWT exp claim checked on every request Session logout The session cookie only Server-side session invalidation

docs.title: Manual Revocation
docs.text:
There is no explicit token revocation endpoint in the community edition. To revoke all tokens for a user:

  1. Change the user's password — this immediately invalidates all outstanding JWTs 

    # Via API (as the user)
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/change \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -b cookies.txt \
  -d '{
    "password": "old_password",
    "new_password": "new_password",
    "new_password_confirm": "new_password"
  }'

    # Via CLI (as admin)
faraday-manage change-password

  2. Rotate the server SECRET_KEY — this invalidates ALL tokens for ALL users (nuclear option, requires server restart)

docs.title: Commercial Persistent Tokens
docs.text: Commercial-edition UserToken records have a revoked boolean field. Setting revoked = True disables the token. These tokens also support expires_at for time-based expiration independent of the JWT mechanism.

docs.title: LDAP
docs.text:
LDAP authentication is a commercial feature that replaces local user authentication with Active Directory / LDAP directory validation.

Key constraints: - Faraday does not support hybrid LDAP + local users — you must choose one - When use_local_roles = True, any AD user can access Faraday - Server restart is required after configuration changes

Configuration via CLI: 

faraday-manage settings -a update ldap

LDAP settings:

Setting Default Description enabled False Enable LDAP authentication server (empty) LDAP server hostname port 389 LDAP server port use_ldaps False Use LDAPS (port 636) use_start_tls False Use STARTTLS on plain port domain (empty) AD domain name domain_dn (empty) Domain distinguished name bind_format CN Bind DN format (CN or DN) bind_dn (empty) Bind distinguished name user_class user LDAP user object class user_attribute sAMAccountName Login attribute group_class group LDAP group object class paginated_fetch False Use paged results use_local_roles True Use Faraday role assignments (vs. LDAP groups) default_local_role admin Default role for new LDAP users admin_group (empty) LDAP group → admin role pentester_group (empty) LDAP group → pentester role asset_owner_group (empty) LDAP group → asset_owner role client_group (empty) LDAP group → client role disconnect_timeout 2.0 LDAP connection timeout (seconds)

LDAP users and API tokens: LDAP-authenticated users can still obtain API tokens via GET /_api/v3/token after establishing a session. The token mechanism is independent of the authentication backend. However, since LDAP users may not have a local password stored, the validation_check claim behavior may differ.

docs.title: SAML
docs.text:
SAML SSO is a commercial feature supporting single sign-on with identity providers such as Auth0, Azure AD, Okta, and Google Workspace.

Key endpoints (SAML):

Endpoint Purpose /_api/saml/login Initiate SAML login flow /_api/saml/acs Assertion Consumer Service (callback) /_api/saml/logout SAML single logout /_api/saml/metadata.xml SP metadata for IdP configuration

Configuration via CLI: 

faraday-manage settings -a update saml

SAML settings:

Setting Default Description enabled False Enable SAML authentication display_name Identity Provider Name shown on login page user_role admin Default role for SAML users admin_group (empty) SAML group → admin role attribute_identifier (empty) SAML attribute for username sso_url http://localhost/saml/login IdP login URL sp_certificate (empty) Service Provider certificate (PEM) sp_private_key (empty) Service Provider private key (PEM) idp_certificate (empty) Identity Provider certificate (PEM)

Generating SP certificates: 

openssl req -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -keyout key.pem -x509 -days 1825 -out certificate.pem

SAML + LDAP constraint: You cannot use both SAML and LDAP simultaneously. You can use SAML alongside local users.

Supported identity providers (with docs): - Auth0 — see [[SAML-Auth0]] - Azure AD — see [[SAML-Azure]] - Okta — see [[SAML-Okta]] - Google Workspace — see [[SAML-Google]]

docs.title: CSRF Protection
docs.text: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection is enforced for session-based authentication only. Requests authenticated via API token, Basic auth, or Agent token bypass CSRF checks.

docs.title: When CSRF Is Required
docs.text:
• Any POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE request using session cookies
  • File uploads (report imports) using session cookies

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Obtain the CSRF token from GET /_api/session: 

    CSRF=$(curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/session \
  -b cookies.txt | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['csrf_token'])")

  2. Include it in state-changing requests via the X-CSRFToken header: 

    curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "X-CSRFToken: $CSRF" \
  -b cookies.txt \
  -d '{"name": "new_workspace"}'

docs.title: Bypassing CSRF
docs.text:
Switch to API token authentication. Token-authenticated requests do not require CSRF tokens:

curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -d '{"name": "new_workspace"}'

docs.title: Token Management
docs.text:
1. Prefer API tokens over Basic auth for production automation — tokens expire, passwords don't
  2. Store tokens securely — tokens grant full user-level access; treat them like passwords
  3. Set appropriate expiration — shorter lifetimes reduce the window of exposure for compromised tokens
  4. Rotate tokens periodically — generate new tokens regularly and discard old ones
  5. Use environment variables for tokens in scripts, never hardcode them

docs.title: Password Security
docs.text:
1. Use strong passwords — Faraday enforces minimum complexity via Flask-Security configuration
  2. Change passwords to revoke tokens — this is the fastest way to invalidate all outstanding tokens
  3. Use faraday-manage change-password for administrative password resets

docs.title: Network Security
docs.text:
1. Always use HTTPS — API tokens and credentials are sent in headers/bodies
  2. Deploy behind NGINX — use faraday-manage generate-nginx-config for a properly configured reverse proxy
  3. Restrict network access — limit API access to trusted networks where possible
  4. Configure idle session timeout — prevent unattended sessions from being hijacked

docs.title: SSO Recommendations
docs.text:
1. Prefer SAML over LDAP when possible — SAML supports federated identity and allows keeping local users as a fallback
  2. Map LDAP/SAML groups to roles — avoid use_local_roles = True with empty group mappings (allows any AD user to access Faraday)
  3. Generate unique SP certificates per Faraday instance
  4. Restart the server after any SSO configuration changes

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text: Symptom Cause Solution 401 Unauthorized on every request Token expired Re-authenticate and request new token 401 after password change Token invalidated by password change Request new token 403 Forbidden with session cookie Missing CSRF token Get CSRF from GET /_api/session 403 Forbidden with agent token Agent not found in database Re-register agent Password reset email not arriving SMTP not configured Configure SMTP via faraday-manage settings -a update smtp LDAP login fails Server not restarted after config change systemctl restart faraday-server Token expires faster/slower than expected Conflicting api_token_expiration values Check actual server.ini value

docs.title: Debugging Authentication
docs.text:
1. Check server logs — authentication failures are logged with details: 

    tail -f ~/.faraday/logs/faraday-server.log

  2. Verify token contents (for debugging only — do not expose in production): 

    # Decode JWT payload (base64, not verified)
echo "$TOKEN" | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | python3 -m json.tool

  3. Test authentication with curl verbose mode: 

    curl -k -v -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN"

  4. Check audit log for token issuance events: 

    grep "requested token" ~/.faraday/logs/faraday-server.log

docs.title: Complete Token-Based Workflow
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# Faraday API Token Authentication — Quick Start

SERVER="https://localhost:5985"
USER="faraday@example.com"
PASS="changeme"

# 1. Obtain token (via Basic auth — single request)
TOKEN=$(curl -k -s --user "$USER:$PASS" "$SERVER/_api/v3/token")

# 2. Use token for all API calls
curl -k -s -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" "$SERVER/_api/v3/ws/"

# 3. Create a workspace
curl -k -s -X POST "$SERVER/_api/v3/ws/" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name": "automated_scan"}'

# 4. Upload a report
curl -k -s -X POST "$SERVER/_api/v3/ws/automated_scan/upload_report" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -F "file=@nmap_results.xml"

docs.title: Environment Variable Pattern
docs.text:
# Store credentials securely
export FARADAY_URL="https://faraday.example.com"
export FARADAY_TOKEN="eyJhbGciOiJIUzUxMiI..."

# Use in scripts
alias fapi='curl -k -s -H "Authorization: Token $FARADAY_TOKEN"'
fapi "$FARADAY_URL/_api/v3/ws/"

docs.text:

  • [[../endpoints/agents]] — Agent management endpoints
  • Faraday Agent Dispatcher — Agent registration
  • Flask-Security Documentation — Underlying auth framework

docs.title: Authentication Endpoint Reference
docs.text:
Faraday Server v5.10.0+ — Complete endpoint specifications for all authentication-related API routes.

Source verification: All endpoints verified against faraday/server/api/modules/session.py, token.py, agent_auth_token.py, websocket_auth.py, and faraday/server/app.py.

docs.title: Endpoint Summary
docs.text:
# Method Path Auth Required Source 1 POST /_api/login No Flask-Security 2 POST /_api/logout Session Flask-Security 3 POST /_api/change Session Flask-Security 4 POST /_api/reset No Flask-Security 5 GET /_api/session Any session.py 6 GET /_api/v3/token Any token.py 7 GET /_api/v3/agent_token Any agent_auth_token.py 8 POST /_api/v3/agent_websocket_token No (public) websocket_auth.py 9 GET/POST /_api/v3/ws/{workspace}/websocket_token Any websocket_auth.py

Note: Endpoints 1–4 are Flask-Security routes and are not included in the OpenAPI spec (faraday_swagger.json). They exist in the running application but are not auto-documented.

docs.title: 1. Login — POST /_api/login
docs.text: Establish an authenticated session.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
POST /_api/login HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "email": "user@example.com",
  "password": "secret"
}
Field Type Required Description email string Yes Username or email address password string Yes Plaintext password

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
200 OK — Session cookie set via Set-Cookie header:

{
  "response": {
    "user": {
      "authentication_token": null
    }
  }
}

401 Unauthorized — Invalid credentials:

{
  "response": {
    "errors": ["Invalid email or password"]
  }
}

docs.title: curl Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -c cookies.txt \
  -d '{"email": "faraday@example.com", "password": "changeme"}'

docs.title: 2. Logout — POST /_api/logout
docs.text: Invalidate the current session.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
POST /_api/logout HTTP/1.1
Cookie: session=<session-cookie>

No request body.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
200 OK:

{}

docs.title: curl Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/logout \
  -b cookies.txt

docs.title: 3. Change Password — POST /_api/change
docs.text: Change the authenticated user's password. Immediately invalidates all outstanding API tokens for this user.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
POST /_api/change HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json
Cookie: session=<session-cookie>

{
  "password": "current_password",
  "new_password": "new_secure_password",
  "new_password_confirm": "new_secure_password"
}
Field Type Required Description password string Yes Current password new_password string Yes New password new_password_confirm string Yes Must match new_password

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
200 OK:

{
  "response": {
    "user": {}
  }
}

400 Bad Request — Validation error:

{
  "response": {
    "errors": {
      "new_password_confirm": ["Passwords do not match"]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: curl Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/change \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -b cookies.txt \
  -d '{
    "password": "changeme",
    "new_password": "N3w$ecur3Pa55!",
    "new_password_confirm": "N3w$ecur3Pa55!"
  }'

docs.title: 4. Reset Password — POST /_api/reset
docs.text: Request a password-reset email. Always returns 200 to prevent account enumeration.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
POST /_api/reset HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "email": "user@example.com"
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
200 OK (always, regardless of whether the email exists):

{}

docs.title: curl Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/reset \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"email": "faraday@example.com"}'

Prerequisite: SMTP must be configured via faraday-manage settings -a update smtp for the reset email to be sent.

docs.title: 5. Get Session Info — GET /_api/session
docs.text: Retrieve session metadata, CSRF token, user preferences, and permissions.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
GET /_api/session HTTP/1.1
Cookie: session=<session-cookie>

Or with token auth:

GET /_api/session HTTP/1.1
Authorization: Token <jwt>

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
200 OK:

{
  "csrf_token": "IjJhNmY4ZDk3ZTBkNGYxYjg5YzRkNThhZGE2MjQzODVhOTc5ZTlmNTki...",
  "preferences": {},
  "permissions": {
    "workspaces": "allowed",
    "vulns": "allowed",
    "hosts": "allowed"
  },
  "user_id": 1
}
Field Type Description csrf_token string CSRF token for session-based state-changing requests preferences object User's stored preferences permissions object Permission map (currently all "allowed") user_id integer Authenticated user's database ID

Source: faraday/server/api/modules/session.py — SessionView.get()

docs.title: curl Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/session \
  -b cookies.txt

docs.title: 6. Get API Token — GET /_api/v3/token
docs.text: Generate a JWT API token for the authenticated user.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
GET /_api/v3/token HTTP/1.1
Cookie: session=<session-cookie>

Or with Basic auth:

GET /_api/v3/token HTTP/1.1
Authorization: Basic <base64(user:password)>

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
200 OK — Returns the raw JWT string (plain text):

eyJhbGciOiJIUzUxMiIsImlhdCI6MTcwOTAzMTU3OCwiZXhwIjoxNzA5MDc0Nzc4fQ.eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoiZjNjYTUyNjYtOWU2ZS00NzQwLWI0NWItN2Y2M2JkZWMzN2UwIiwidmFsaWRhdGlvbl9jaGVjayI6IiQ1JHJvdW5kcz01MzUwMDAkVDZ3cy9ZMGFHUW10UnEzSSRpRS5OSG1oaDNWR0h4VXIudVpRNGRnaEVVUXFiS09pWGg5VGdrTURtQXIwIn0.gu6AgQ-iIvdnrHBphBfzWopn5B21D_RvTFvltcoMnksAj0eHhfh7dkJW4GfjhZPQd02RBAsb3vvUUCA0fORvfw

Audit trail: Token requests are logged: User [username] requested token from IP [ip] at [timestamp]

Source: faraday/server/api/modules/token.py — TokenAuthView.get()

docs.title: curl Examples
docs.text:
# Via session cookie
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/token \
  -b cookies.txt

# Via Basic auth (one-step token retrieval)
curl -k -s --user "faraday@example.com:changeme" \
  https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/token

docs.title: 7. Get Agent Registration Token — GET /_api/v3/agent_token
docs.text: Generate a TOTP code for agent registration.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
GET /_api/v3/agent_token HTTP/1.1
Authorization: Token <jwt>

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
200 OK:

{
  "token": "482915",
  "expires_in": 42,
  "total_duration": 60
}
Field Type Description token string Current TOTP code expires_in integer Seconds until code expires total_duration integer TOTP interval (default 60s)

Source: faraday/server/api/modules/agent_auth_token.py

docs.title: curl Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/agent_token \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN"

docs.title: 8. Agent WebSocket Token — POST /_api/v3/agent_websocket_token
docs.text: Obtain a short-lived websocket token for agent communication. This is a public endpoint (is_public = True) — authentication is handled at the application level via the agent's persistent token in the request body.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
POST /_api/v3/agent_websocket_token HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "token": "<agent-persistent-token>"
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
200 OK:

{
  "token": "signed-websocket-token..."
}

403 Forbidden — Agent token not found.

Source: faraday/server/api/modules/websocket_auth.py

docs.title: 9. Workspace WebSocket Token — GET/POST /_api/v3/ws/{workspace}/websocket_token
docs.text: Obtain a workspace-scoped websocket token for real-time event subscriptions.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
GET /_api/v3/ws/my_workspace/websocket_token HTTP/1.1
Authorization: Token <jwt>

docs.title: Path Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description workspace string Yes Target workspace name

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
200 OK:

{
  "token": "workspace-websocket-token..."
}
Status Meaning 200 Token returned 401 Not authenticated 403 No access to workspace 404 Workspace not found

Source: faraday/server/api/modules/websocket_auth.py

docs.title: curl Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X GET \
  https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/my_workspace/websocket_token \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN"

docs.title: Common Error Responses
docs.text: Status Body Cause 400 {"response": {"errors": {...}}} Malformed request, validation failure 401 401: Unauthorized Missing or invalid credentials/token 403 403: Forbidden Valid auth but insufficient access 404 404: Not Found Resource not found (e.g., workspace)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• [[README]] — Complete authentication guide
  • [[token-lifecycle]] — JWT internals and validation flow
  • [[sso-integration]] — SSO/LDAP setup guides

docs.title: Authentication & Session API
docs.text:
Faraday Security REST API v3 -- Version 5.10.0

This document covers every endpoint related to authentication, session management, and token issuance in the Faraday Server API.

docs.title: Authentication Methods Overview
docs.text: Faraday supports three distinct authentication mechanisms. The method you choose depends on your use case.

docs.title: 1. Session Cookies
docs.text:
The primary interactive method. A client authenticates via POST /_api/login and receives an HTTP-only session cookie. All subsequent requests include this cookie automatically. A CSRF token (obtained from GET /_api/session) is required for state-changing requests when using session-based auth.

Best for: Browser-based clients, web UI, interactive scripts that maintain a cookie jar.

docs.title: 2. API Tokens
docs.text:
A bearer-style token retrieved from GET /_api/v3/token after an authenticated session has been established. The token is a base64-encoded string passed in the Authorization header.

Authorization: Token <base64-token>

API tokens have a finite lifetime indicated by expires_in (seconds remaining) and total_duration (original lifetime in seconds). When a token expires the client must re-authenticate and request a new one.

Best for: Automation, CI/CD pipelines, scripts, third-party integrations.

docs.title: 3. Agent Tokens
docs.text:
A separate token class used exclusively by Faraday Agents (dispatchers and executors). Retrieved from GET /_api/v3/agent_token. Agent tokens authorize agent self-registration and communication over the WebSocket channel.

Best for: Faraday Agent Dispatcher instances registering with the server.

docs.title: 1. Login
docs.text:
Authenticate a user and establish a server-side session.

Property Value Method POST Path /_api/login Auth Public (no auth required) Content-Type application/json

Note: This is a Flask-Security endpoint. It is not listed in the OpenAPI spec (faraday_swagger.json) but exists in the running application.

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: Field Type Required Description email string Yes User's email address (login). password string Yes User's plaintext password.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
Status Meaning 200 Login successful. Session cookie is set. 401 Invalid credentials.

On success the server sets an HTTP-only session cookie (typically named session) in the Set-Cookie response header.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Login and save the session cookie to cookies.txt
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -c cookies.txt \
  -d '{
    "email": "faraday@example.com",
    "password": "changeme"
  }'

Success (200):

{
  "response": {
    "user": {
      "authentication_token": null
    }
  }
}

Failure (401):

{
  "response": {
    "errors": ["Invalid email or password"]
  }
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The session cookie is HTTP-only and should be stored in a cookie jar (-c cookies.txt in curl) for reuse in later requests.
  • The server may also return a CSRF cookie. For state-changing requests (POST, PUT, DELETE) that use session auth, you should obtain the CSRF token from GET /_api/session and include it as a header.

docs.title: 2. Logout
docs.text:
Invalidate the current session and clear the server-side session data.

Property Value Method POST Path /_api/logout Auth Requires session cookie

Note: Flask-Security endpoint. Not in the OpenAPI spec but exists in the running application.

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Response
docs.text: Status Meaning 200 Session invalidated.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Logout using the saved session cookie
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/logout \
  -b cookies.txt

Success (200):

{}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• After logout the session cookie is no longer valid. Any subsequent requests using the same cookie will receive a 401 response.
  • The cookie file should be discarded or overwritten after logout.

docs.title: 3. Change Password
docs.text:
Change the password for the currently authenticated user.

Property Value Method POST Path /_api/change Auth Requires session cookie Content-Type application/json

Note: Flask-Security endpoint. Not in the OpenAPI spec but exists in the running application.

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: Field Type Required Description password string Yes Current password. new_password string Yes Desired new password. new_password_confirm string Yes Must match new_password exactly.

docs.title: Response
docs.text: Status Meaning 200 Password changed successfully. 400 Validation error (mismatch, weak password, etc.). 401 Current password incorrect or session not valid.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/change \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -b cookies.txt \
  -d '{
    "password": "changeme",
    "new_password": "N3w$ecur3Pa55!",
    "new_password_confirm": "N3w$ecur3Pa55!"
  }'

Success (200):

{
  "response": {
    "user": {}
  }
}

Validation Error (400):

{
  "response": {
    "errors": {
      "new_password_confirm": ["Passwords do not match"]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The session remains valid after a password change; you do not need to re-authenticate.
  • Password complexity requirements are governed by the server's Flask-Security configuration.

docs.title: 4. Reset Password (Forgot Password)
docs.text:
Request a password-reset email for the given account.

Property Value Method POST Path /_api/reset Auth Public (no auth required) Content-Type application/json

Note: Flask-Security endpoint. Not in the OpenAPI spec but exists in the running application.

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: Field Type Required Description email string Yes Email address of the account to reset.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
Status Meaning 200 Request accepted. Always returns 200 to prevent enumeration.

The server always responds with 200 regardless of whether the email address exists. This prevents attackers from enumerating valid accounts.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/reset \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "email": "faraday@example.com"
  }'

Response (200 -- always):

{}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The actual password reset is completed by following the link in the email, which contains a time-limited token.
  • If the SMTP server is not configured the request will succeed silently but no email will be sent. Check the server logs if resets are not arriving.

docs.title: 5. Get Session Info
docs.text:
Retrieve session metadata including the CSRF token required for state-changing requests.

Property Value Method GET Path /_api/session Auth Requires auth

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
Status Meaning 200 Session info returned. 401 Not authenticated.

Response Schema:

Field Type Description csrf_token string CSRF token for the current session.

The response may also include information about the current user depending on the server version.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/session \
  -b cookies.txt

Success (200):

{
  "csrf_token": "IjJhNmY4ZDk3ZTBkNGYxYjg5YzRkNThhZGE2MjQzODVhOTc5ZTlmNTki.Z8BmJQ.abc123..."
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• File uploads require the CSRF token. Before performing any file upload (e.g., importing a report), call this endpoint and include the returned token as the X-CSRFToken header or in the request body.
  • When using API token authentication (Authorization: Token ...) instead of session cookies, CSRF protection is typically bypassed. The CSRF token is only relevant for session-cookie-based auth.
  • This endpoint is the canonical way to verify that a session is still valid.

docs.title: 6. Get API Token
docs.text:
Obtain a bearer token that can be used for API authentication without session cookies.

Property Value Method GET Path /_api/v3/token Auth Requires auth (session cookie)

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
Status Meaning 200 Token returned successfully. 401 Not authenticated.

Response Schema (Token):

Field Type Description token string Base64-encoded API token. expires_in integer Seconds remaining until the token expires. total_duration integer Original token lifetime in seconds.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# First login to get a session cookie
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -c cookies.txt \
  -d '{"email": "faraday@example.com", "password": "changeme"}'

# Then retrieve the API token
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/token \
  -b cookies.txt

Success (200):

{
  "token": "ZmFyYWRheUBleGFtcGxlLmNvbTpNVEl6TkRVMk56ZzVNREV5TXpRMU5qYzRPVEF4...",
  "expires_in": 43200,
  "total_duration": 43200
}

Using the token in subsequent requests:

# Use the token instead of session cookies
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -H "Authorization: Token ZmFyYWRheUBleGFtcGxlLmNvbTpNVEl6TkRVMk56ZzVNREV5TXpRMU5qYzRPVEF4..."

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The token is tied to the authenticated user and carries the same permissions.
  • When the token expires, re-authenticate and call this endpoint again to get a fresh token.
  • API token auth does not require CSRF tokens for state-changing requests.
  • Store the token securely -- it grants full access to the API as the authenticated user.

docs.title: 7. Get Agent Token
docs.text:
Retrieve a token used for Faraday Agent self-registration.

Property Value Method GET Path /_api/v3/agent_token Auth Requires auth

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
Status Meaning 200 Agent token returned successfully. 401 Not authenticated.

Response Schema (AgentAuthToken):

Field Type Description token string Agent registration token. expires_in integer Seconds remaining until the token expires. total_duration integer Original token lifetime in seconds.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/agent_token \
  -b cookies.txt

Success (200):

{
  "token": "YWdlbnRfdG9rZW5fZXhhbXBsZV8xMjM0NTY3ODkw...",
  "expires_in": 86400,
  "total_duration": 86400
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This token is used by the Faraday Agent Dispatcher during its registration handshake with the server. It is distinct from the user API token.
  • Pass this token to the agent dispatcher's configuration file or environment variable so the agent can register itself with the Faraday server.
  • The token scope is limited to agent registration operations and cannot be used for general API access.

docs.title: 8. Agent Websocket Token
docs.text:
Obtain a websocket authentication token for agent-to-server communication.

Property Value Method POST Path /_api/v3/agent_websocket_token Auth Public (is_public = True)

This endpoint is marked as public because agents call it using their own agent-level credentials (not a user session).

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: The request body depends on the agent's registration state and may include agent identification fields. Consult the Faraday Agent Dispatcher source for the exact payload.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
Status Meaning 200 Websocket token returned. 401 Agent credentials invalid.

Response Schema:

Field Type Description token string Websocket authentication token for the agent.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/agent_websocket_token \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "token": "YWdlbnRfdG9rZW5fZXhhbXBsZV8xMjM0NTY3ODkw..."
  }'

Success (200):

{
  "token": "d3NfdG9rZW5fZXhhbXBsZV9hYmNkZWYxMjM0NTY..."
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This endpoint is designed to be called by agent processes, not by human users or general-purpose scripts.
  • The returned token is short-lived and scoped to a single WebSocket connection.
  • The is_public = True flag in the code means Flask-Security's login_required decorator is not applied. Authentication is handled at the application level using the agent token in the request body.

docs.title: 9. Websocket Token (Workspace-Scoped)
docs.text:
Obtain a websocket authentication token scoped to a specific workspace, used for real-time event subscriptions.

Property Value Method GET or POST Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/websocket_token Auth Requires auth

docs.title: Path Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description workspace_name string Yes Name of the target workspace.

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: None (for GET). The POST variant accepts the same path parameter.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
Status Meaning 200 Websocket token returned. 401 Not authenticated. 403 No access to the specified workspace. 404 Workspace not found.

Response Schema:

Field Type Description token string Websocket token scoped to the given workspace.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# GET variant
curl -k -s -X GET \
  https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/my_workspace/websocket_token \
  -b cookies.txt

Success (200):

{
  "token": "d3Nfd29ya3NwYWNlX3Rva2VuX2V4YW1wbGU..."
}

# POST variant (equivalent)
curl -k -s -X POST \
  https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/my_workspace/websocket_token \
  -b cookies.txt

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The websocket token is used to open a WebSocket connection to the Faraday server for receiving real-time notifications about changes in the specified workspace (new vulnerabilities, host changes, command execution updates, etc.).
  • The user must have at least read access to the workspace; otherwise the server returns 403.
  • A typical WebSocket connection URL looks like: 
    wss://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/my_workspace/websocket?token=<token>

docs.title: Quick Reference: Session Management with curl
docs.text:
Below is a complete workflow demonstrating session-based authentication from the command line.

# 1. Login and store cookies
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -c cookies.txt \
  -d '{"email": "faraday@example.com", "password": "changeme"}'

# 2. Retrieve CSRF token
CSRF=$(curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/session \
  -b cookies.txt | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['csrf_token'])")

# 3. Make authenticated requests (read -- no CSRF needed)
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -b cookies.txt

# 4. Make state-changing requests (write -- CSRF required)
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "X-CSRFToken: $CSRF" \
  -b cookies.txt \
  -d '{"name": "new_workspace"}'

# 5. Retrieve an API token for non-interactive use
TOKEN=$(curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/token \
  -b cookies.txt | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['token'])")

# 6. Use the API token (no cookies or CSRF needed)
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN"

# 7. Logout when done
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/logout \
  -b cookies.txt

# 8. Clean up
rm -f cookies.txt

docs.title: Quick Reference: API Token Authentication
docs.text:
For scripts and automation, token-based auth avoids the need for cookie management and CSRF tokens.

# Obtain a token (one-time, requires login first)
curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -c cookies.txt \
  -d '{"email": "faraday@example.com", "password": "changeme"}'

TOKEN=$(curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/token \
  -b cookies.txt | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['token'])")

rm -f cookies.txt

# All subsequent requests use the token
curl -k -s -X GET https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN"

curl -k -s -X POST https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/ \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -d '{"name": "automated_workspace"}'

docs.title: Error Responses
docs.text:
All authentication endpoints follow a consistent error format.

Status Meaning Typical Cause 400 Bad Request Malformed JSON, missing required fields. 401 Unauthorized Missing or invalid credentials/token/cookie. 403 Forbidden Valid auth but insufficient permissions. 404 Not Found Workspace does not exist (websocket token). 500 Internal Server Error Server-side issue (check logs).

Generic 401 response:

{
  "response": {
    "errors": ["Unauthorized"]
  }
}

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• [[README]] -- API endpoints index
  • [[workspaces]] -- Workspace management endpoints (require auth)
  • Faraday Agent Dispatcher -- Agent registration using agent tokens
  • Flask-Security Documentation -- Underlying auth framework

docs.title: Bulk Operations API
docs.text:
Base URL: https://localhost:5985 API Version: v3 Authentication: Cookie-based session or API token (Authorization: Token <token>) Content-Type: application/json

Faraday provides three categories of bulk operations for efficiently managing large datasets: Bulk Create (importing hosts, services, and vulnerabilities in a single request), Bulk Delete (removing multiple resources by ID), and Bulk Update (modifying multiple resources with the same field values). This page documents the bulk operation patterns and their supported resources.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• 1. Bulk Create
  • BulkCreate Schema
  • BulkCommand Schema
  • HostBulk Schema
  • BulkService Schema
  • Bulk Create Endpoint
  • 2. Bulk Delete
  • Bulk Delete Pattern
  • Supported Resources
  • 3. Bulk Update
  • Bulk Update Pattern
  • Supported Resources
  • Common Error Codes

docs.title: 1. Bulk Create
docs.text: The Bulk Create endpoint allows importing an entire scan result -- hosts, services, and vulnerabilities -- in a single API call. This is the same mechanism used internally when Faraday processes tool reports (e.g., Nmap, Nessus). It is the most efficient way to programmatically populate a workspace with scan data.

docs.title: BulkCreate Schema
docs.text: Property Type Required Description hosts array of HostBulk yes Array of host objects to create, each optionally containing nested services and vulnerabilities. command BulkCommand yes Metadata about the command/tool that produced these results. execution_id integer no ID of an agent execution that triggered this import (used by the agent dispatcher).

docs.title: BulkCommand Schema
docs.text: Property Type Required Description command string yes The full command string that was executed (e.g., "nmap -sV -p1-65535 192.168.1.0/24"). tool string yes Name of the tool (e.g., "nmap", "nessus", "burp"). start_date datetime yes ISO 8601 timestamp when the command started execution. duration float no Execution duration in seconds. ip string no IP address from which the command was executed. hostname string no Hostname of the machine that executed the command. params string no Command parameters/arguments. user string no Username who executed the command. creator string no Creator identifier. import_source string no Source of the import (e.g., "report", "agent", "api").

docs.title: HostBulk Schema
docs.text:
Extends the standard Host schema with nested child resources.

Property Type Required Description ip string yes IPv4 or IPv6 address. description string no Free-text description of the host. hostnames array of strings no DNS hostnames associated with the host. os string no Operating system fingerprint. mac string no MAC address. vulnerabilities array of objects no Vulnerabilities directly associated with the host (not tied to a specific service). Each object follows the standard Vulnerability creation schema. services array of BulkService no Services discovered on the host.

docs.title: BulkService Schema
docs.text:
Extends the standard Service schema with nested vulnerabilities.

Property Type Required Description name string no Service name (e.g., "ssh", "http"). port integer yes Port number. protocol string yes Transport protocol ("tcp" or "udp"). status string no Port status (e.g., "open", "closed", "filtered"). Defaults to "open". version string no Service version string (e.g., "OpenSSH 8.9"). description string no Free-text description. vulnerabilities array of objects no Vulnerabilities associated with this service. Each object follows the standard Vulnerability creation schema.

docs.title: Bulk Create Endpoint
docs.text:
Create hosts, services, and vulnerabilities in a single request.

Method POST Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/bulk_create Auth Required

Path Parameters:

Parameter Type Description workspace_name string Target workspace slug.

Request Body:

{
  "hosts": [
    {
      "ip": "192.168.1.1",
      "description": "Web server",
      "hostnames": ["web.example.com"],
      "os": "Linux 5.15",
      "services": [
        {
          "name": "http",
          "port": 80,
          "protocol": "tcp",
          "status": "open",
          "version": "Apache 2.4.54",
          "vulnerabilities": [
            {
              "name": "Apache HTTP Server Path Traversal",
              "severity": "critical",
              "desc": "CVE-2021-41773: Path traversal in Apache HTTP Server 2.4.49.",
              "refs": ["https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2021-41773"],
              "type": "Vulnerability",
              "status": "open"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "name": "ssh",
          "port": 22,
          "protocol": "tcp",
          "status": "open",
          "version": "OpenSSH 8.9",
          "vulnerabilities": []
        }
      ],
      "vulnerabilities": [
        {
          "name": "Outdated OS Kernel",
          "severity": "med",
          "desc": "The host is running a kernel version with known vulnerabilities.",
          "type": "Vulnerability",
          "status": "open"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "ip": "192.168.1.2",
      "description": "Database server",
      "os": "Linux 5.15",
      "services": [
        {
          "name": "postgresql",
          "port": 5432,
          "protocol": "tcp",
          "status": "open",
          "version": "PostgreSQL 14.5"
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "command": {
    "command": "nmap -sV -p1-65535 192.168.1.0/24",
    "tool": "nmap",
    "start_date": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
    "duration": 345.2,
    "hostname": "scanner.example.com",
    "ip": "10.0.0.5",
    "user": "admin",
    "import_source": "api"
  }
}

Response: 201 Created

{
  "command_id": 15,
  "created": {
    "hosts": 2,
    "services": 3,
    "vulnerabilities": 2
  }
}

Example:

curl -s -X POST \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/bulk_create" \
  -H "Authorization: Token YOUR_API_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -k \
  -d '{
    "hosts": [
      {
        "ip": "192.168.1.1",
        "description": "Web server",
        "os": "Linux",
        "services": [
          {
            "name": "http",
            "port": 80,
            "protocol": "tcp",
            "status": "open",
            "vulnerabilities": [
              {
                "name": "Directory Listing Enabled",
                "severity": "low",
                "desc": "Web server exposes directory listings.",
                "type": "Vulnerability",
                "status": "open"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    ],
    "command": {
      "command": "nmap -sV 192.168.1.1",
      "tool": "nmap",
      "start_date": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00"
    }
  }'

Error Codes:

Code Reason 400 Validation error in one or more objects (e.g., missing required fields, invalid severity value). 401 Missing or invalid authentication. 403 User lacks permission for this workspace. 404 Workspace not found.

Notes:

  • If a host with the same IP already exists in the workspace, the existing host is updated (merged) rather than creating a duplicate.
  • If a service with the same port/protocol already exists on the host, it is updated rather than duplicated.
  • Vulnerability deduplication is based on name, desc, and associated host/service.
  • The command object is required and creates a Command record that appears in the workspace's command history.

docs.title: 2. Bulk Delete
docs.text: Bulk delete removes multiple resources in a single request by specifying an array of IDs.

docs.title: Bulk Delete Pattern
docs.text:
Method DELETE Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/{resource} or /_api/v3/{resource} Auth Required

Request Body:

{
  "ids": [1, 2, 3]
}

Response: 204 No Content

Some resources return 200 OK with a count:

{
  "deleted": 3
}

Example (workspaced):

curl -s -X DELETE \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/vulns" \
  -H "Authorization: Token YOUR_API_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -k \
  -d '{"ids": [10, 11, 12]}'

Example (global):

curl -s -X DELETE \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/vulnerability_template" \
  -H "Authorization: Token YOUR_API_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -k \
  -d '{"ids": [1, 2, 3]}'

Error Codes:

Code Reason 400 Invalid request body or empty ids array. 401 Missing or invalid authentication. 403 User lacks permission for the workspace or resource. 404 Workspace not found (for workspaced endpoints).

docs.title: Bulk Delete Supported Resources
docs.text: Resource Workspaced Path Global Path Hosts DELETE /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/hosts DELETE /_api/v3/hosts Services DELETE /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/services DELETE /_api/v3/services Vulnerabilities DELETE /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/vulns DELETE /_api/v3/vulns Credentials DELETE /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/credential -- Comments DELETE /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment -- Commands DELETE /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands -- Vulnerability Templates -- DELETE /_api/v3/vulnerability_template Custom Fields -- DELETE /_api/v3/custom_fields_schema Search Filters -- DELETE /_api/v3/searchfilter Agents -- DELETE /_api/v3/agents Agent Schedules -- DELETE /_api/v3/agents_schedule

docs.title: 3. Bulk Update
docs.text: Bulk update modifies multiple resources with the same set of field values in a single request.

docs.title: Bulk Update Pattern
docs.text:
Method PATCH Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/{resource} or /_api/v3/{resource} Auth Required

Request Body:

{
  "ids": [1, 2, 3],
  "update": {
    "field_name": "new_value"
  }
}

The update object contains only the fields to be changed. Omitted fields remain unchanged on each resource.

Response: 200 OK

{
  "updated": 3
}

Example (workspaced -- update vulnerability severity):

curl -s -X PATCH \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/vulns" \
  -H "Authorization: Token YOUR_API_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -k \
  -d '{"ids": [10, 11, 12], "update": {"severity": "critical"}}'

Example (workspaced -- mark hosts as owned):

curl -s -X PATCH \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/hosts" \
  -H "Authorization: Token YOUR_API_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -k \
  -d '{"ids": [1, 2], "update": {"owned": true}}'

Example (global -- update template exploitation level):

curl -s -X PATCH \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/vulnerability_template" \
  -H "Authorization: Token YOUR_API_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -k \
  -d '{"ids": [5, 6, 7], "update": {"exploitation": "high"}}'

Error Codes:

Code Reason 400 Invalid request body, empty ids, or invalid fields in update. 401 Missing or invalid authentication. 403 User lacks permission for the workspace or resource. 404 Workspace not found (for workspaced endpoints).

docs.title: Bulk Update Supported Resources
docs.text: Resource Workspaced Path Global Path Hosts PATCH /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/hosts PATCH /_api/v3/hosts Services PATCH /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/services PATCH /_api/v3/services Vulnerabilities PATCH /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/vulns PATCH /_api/v3/vulns Vulnerability Templates -- PATCH /_api/v3/vulnerability_template Search Filters -- PATCH /_api/v3/searchfilter

docs.title: Common Error Codes
docs.text: HTTP Status Meaning 200 Success. Response body contains the operation result (e.g., counts of created/updated/deleted objects). 201 Created. Resources were successfully created (returned by some bulk create operations). 204 No Content. Resources were deleted successfully. 400 Bad Request. Malformed JSON, missing required fields, empty ids array, or invalid field values. 401 Unauthorized. Authentication token is missing, expired, or invalid. 403 Forbidden. Authenticated user does not have permission for the requested workspace or resource. 404 Not Found. The workspace does not exist. 409 Conflict. One or more resources conflict with existing data (e.g., duplicate IP in bulk create). 500 Internal Server Error. Unexpected server-side failure.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• All curl examples include -k to skip TLS certificate verification for local development. Remove this flag in production environments with valid certificates.
  • The Bulk Create endpoint (POST /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/bulk_create) is the primary mechanism used by Faraday's plugin system when importing tool reports. It handles deduplication automatically.
  • Bulk delete on hosts cascades to their services and vulnerabilities. Deleting a service cascades to its vulnerabilities.
  • The ids array in bulk delete and bulk update requests must not be empty. An empty array returns 400.
  • Bulk update only modifies the fields specified in the update object. All other fields on each resource remain unchanged.
  • Not all resources support both bulk delete and bulk update. Refer to the supported resources tables above for the complete list.
  • For resources that support both workspaced and global bulk operations, the global endpoints operate across all workspaces accessible to the authenticated user.

docs.title: Commands API
docs.text:
API Version: v3 Spec Version: Faraday 5.10.0 Base URL: https://localhost:5985 Authentication: Cookie-based session or API token (Authorization: Token <token>) Content-Type: application/json

Commands represent tool executions (scans, imports, manual runs) tracked by Faraday. Each command records which tool was invoked, when it ran, and what parameters were used. Commands are the parent context for assets created during an import or scan -- hosts, services, and vulnerabilities are linked back to the command that discovered them.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Command Schema
  • Workspaced Endpoints
  • List Commands
  • Create Command
  • Get Command
  • Update Command (Full)
  • Update Command (Partial)
  • Delete Command
  • Count Commands
  • Activity Feed
  • Last Command
  • Global Endpoints
  • List Commands (Global)
  • Get Command (Global)
  • Error Responses
  • See Also

docs.title: Command Schema
docs.text:
Property Type Required Read-Only Description _id integer -- yes Internal document ID command string yes -- The full command string that was executed (e.g., "nmap -sV 192.168.1.0/24") duration number -- -- Execution duration in seconds (may be null if still running or unknown) itime datetime yes -- Command start time in ISO 8601 format ip string -- -- IP address from which the command was executed hostname string -- -- Hostname of the machine that executed the command params string -- -- Additional parameters or notes associated with the command user string -- -- Username of the person or process that ran the command creator string -- yes Username of the Faraday user who created this record workspace string -- yes Name of the workspace this command belongs to tool string yes -- Tool identifier (e.g., "nmap", "burp", "nessus") import_source string (enum) -- -- Source of the import. One of: "report", "shell", "agent" warnings string (nullable) -- -- Warning messages generated during import (max 250 characters) metadata object -- yes Timestamps and tracking data (create_time, update_time, update_user)

Required fields for creation: command, itime, tool

docs.title: Example Command Object
docs.text:
{
  "_id": 15,
  "command": "nmap -sV -O 192.168.1.0/24",
  "duration": 342.5,
  "itime": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
  "ip": "10.0.0.5",
  "hostname": "scanner.internal",
  "params": "-sV -O",
  "user": "admin",
  "creator": "admin",
  "workspace": "pentest_q1",
  "tool": "nmap",
  "import_source": "shell",
  "metadata": {
    "create_time": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
    "creator": "admin",
    "owner": "admin",
    "update_action": 0,
    "update_time": "2026-02-27T10:06:00+00:00",
    "update_user": "admin"
  }
}

docs.title: Workspaced Endpoints
docs.text:
All workspaced endpoints operate under the path prefix /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands. Replace {workspace_name} with the target workspace slug.

# Method Path Summary 1 GET /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands List commands 2 POST /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands Create command 3 GET /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands/{object_id} Get command 4 PUT /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands/{object_id} Full update 5 PATCH /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands/{object_id} Partial update 6 DELETE /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands/{object_id} Delete command 7 GET /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands/count Count commands 8 GET /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands/activity_feed Activity feed 9 GET /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/commands/last Last command

docs.title: 1. List Commands
docs.text:
Retrieve a paginated list of all commands in a workspace.

Method GET Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands Auth Required

Query Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description page integer 0 Page number (zero-indexed) page_size integer 20 Number of results per page sort string -- Field to sort by sort_dir string asc Sort direction (asc or desc)

Response: 200 OK

{
  "count": 42,
  "data": [
    { "...command object..." }
  ]
}

Example:

curl -s -X GET \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands?page=0&page_size=10" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -k

Error Codes:

Code Reason 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for this workspace 404 Workspace not found

docs.title: 2. Create Command
docs.text:
Create a new command record in a workspace. This is typically called when a tool execution begins or when importing scan results.

Method POST Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands Auth Required Content-Type application/json

Request Body:

Field Type Required Description command string yes Full command string tool string yes Tool identifier itime datetime yes Start time (ISO 8601) duration number no Execution duration in seconds ip string no Source IP address hostname string no Source hostname params string no Additional parameters user string no User who ran the command import_source string no Import source identifier

Response: 201 Created

Returns the newly created command object.

Example:

curl -s -X POST \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -k \
  -d '{
    "command": "nmap -sV 192.168.1.0/24",
    "tool": "nmap",
    "itime": "2026-02-27T10:00:00",
    "ip": "10.0.0.5",
    "hostname": "scanner.internal",
    "params": "-sV",
    "user": "admin",
    "import_source": "shell"
  }'

Success (201):

{
  "_id": 16,
  "command": "nmap -sV 192.168.1.0/24",
  "tool": "nmap",
  "itime": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
  "duration": null,
  "ip": "10.0.0.5",
  "hostname": "scanner.internal",
  "params": "-sV",
  "user": "admin",
  "creator": "admin",
  "workspace": "pentest_q1",
  "import_source": "shell",
  "metadata": {
    "create_time": "2026-02-27T10:00:05+00:00",
    "creator": "admin",
    "owner": "admin",
    "update_action": 0,
    "update_time": "2026-02-27T10:00:05+00:00",
    "update_user": "admin"
  }
}

Error Codes:

Code Reason 400 Validation error (missing command, tool, or itime) 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for this workspace 404 Workspace not found

docs.title: 3. Get Command
docs.text:
Retrieve a single command by its ID.

Method GET Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands/{object_id} Auth Required

Path Parameters:

Parameter Type Description workspace_name string Workspace slug object_id integer Command ID

Response: 200 OK

Returns the full command object.

Example:

curl -s -X GET \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands/15" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -k

Error Codes:

Code Reason 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for this workspace 404 Command or workspace not found

docs.title: 4. Update Command (Full)
docs.text:
Replace all mutable fields of a command. Fields not included in the request body are reset to their defaults.

Method PUT Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands/{object_id} Auth Required Content-Type application/json

Request Body:

Full command object with all required fields (command, tool, itime).

Response: 200 OK

Returns the updated command object.

Example:

curl -s -X PUT \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands/15" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -k \
  -d '{
    "command": "nmap -sV -O 192.168.1.0/24",
    "tool": "nmap",
    "itime": "2026-02-27T10:00:00",
    "duration": 342.5,
    "ip": "10.0.0.5",
    "hostname": "scanner.internal",
    "params": "-sV -O",
    "user": "admin",
    "import_source": "shell"
  }'

Error Codes:

Code Reason 400 Validation error (missing required fields) 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for this workspace 404 Command or workspace not found

docs.title: 5. Update Command (Partial)
docs.text:
Update only the specified fields of a command. Omitted fields remain unchanged.

Method PATCH Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands/{object_id} Auth Required Content-Type application/json

Request Body:

Only the fields to be updated.

Response: 200 OK

Returns the updated command object.

Example:

curl -s -X PATCH \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands/15" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -k \
  -d '{"duration": 342.5}'

Success (200):

{
  "_id": 15,
  "command": "nmap -sV -O 192.168.1.0/24",
  "tool": "nmap",
  "itime": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
  "duration": 342.5,
  "ip": "10.0.0.5",
  "hostname": "scanner.internal",
  "params": "-sV -O",
  "user": "admin",
  "creator": "admin",
  "workspace": "pentest_q1",
  "import_source": "shell",
  "metadata": {
    "create_time": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
    "creator": "admin",
    "owner": "admin",
    "update_action": 0,
    "update_time": "2026-02-27T10:07:00+00:00",
    "update_user": "admin"
  }
}

Error Codes:

Code Reason 400 Validation error 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for this workspace 404 Command or workspace not found

docs.title: 6. Delete Command
docs.text:
Delete a single command by ID.

Method DELETE Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands/{object_id} Auth Required

Path Parameters:

Parameter Type Description workspace_name string Workspace slug object_id integer Command ID

Response: 204 No Content

No response body.

Example:

curl -s -X DELETE \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands/15" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -k
# Returns HTTP 204 with empty body

Error Codes:

Code Reason 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for this workspace 404 Command or workspace not found

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: • Deleting a command does not cascade-delete hosts, services, or vulnerabilities that were created by that command. Only the command record itself is removed.

docs.title: 7. Count Commands
docs.text:
Return the total number of commands in a workspace, optionally grouped by a field.

Method GET Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands/count Auth Required

Query Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description group_by string (none) Field to group counts by (e.g., tool, user)

Response: 200 OK

Without group_by:

{
  "count": 42
}

With group_by=tool:

{
  "groups": [
    {"count": 18, "tool": "nmap"},
    {"count": 12, "tool": "nessus"},
    {"count": 7, "tool": "burp"},
    {"count": 5, "tool": "zap"}
  ]
}

Example:

# Total count
curl -s -X GET \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands/count" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -k

# Grouped by tool
curl -s -X GET \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands/count?group_by=tool" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -k

Error Codes:

Code Reason 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for this workspace 404 Workspace not found

docs.title: 8. Activity Feed
docs.text:
Get a summary of the most recently executed commands in a workspace, enriched with counts of assets created by each command (hosts, services, and vulnerabilities by severity).

Method GET Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands/activity_feed Auth Required

Query Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description page integer 0 Page number (zero-indexed) page_size integer 20 Number of results per page

Response: 200 OK

Returns a paginated list of command summaries with aggregated asset creation counts.

{
  "count": 5,
  "data": [
    {
      "_id": 15,
      "command": "nmap -sV 192.168.1.0/24",
      "tool": "nmap",
      "user": "admin",
      "itime": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
      "sum_created_hosts": 24,
      "sum_created_services": 87,
      "sum_created_vulnerabilities": 156,
      "sum_created_vulnerability_critical": 3,
      "sum_created_vulnerability_high": 12,
      "sum_created_vulnerability_medium": 45,
      "sum_created_vulnerability_low": 61,
      "sum_created_vulnerability_info": 35,
      "sum_created_vulnerability_unclassified": 0
    }
  ]
}

Example:

curl -s -X GET \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands/activity_feed?page=0&page_size=5" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -k

Error Codes:

Code Reason 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for this workspace 404 Workspace not found

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The activity feed is commonly used by the Faraday web UI dashboard to display a timeline of recent scan activity with impact metrics.
  • The sum_created_* fields reflect the number of assets that were created during the execution of each command.
  • This endpoint overlaps with the [[activities]] API but provides a command-centric view rather than a standalone activity resource.

docs.title: 9. Last Command
docs.text:
Retrieve the most recently executed command in a workspace, based on the itime (start time) field.

Method GET Path /_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/commands/last Auth Required

Response: 200 OK

Returns a single command object (the most recent by itime).

{
  "_id": 15,
  "command": "nmap -sV 192.168.1.0/24",
  "tool": "nmap",
  "itime": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
  "duration": 342.5,
  "user": "admin",
  "creator": "admin",
  "workspace": "pentest_q1"
}

Example:

curl -s -X GET \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/ws/pentest_q1/commands/last" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -k

Error Codes:

Code Reason 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for this workspace 404 Workspace not found or no commands exist in workspace

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• If the workspace contains no commands, the server returns 404.
  • This is a convenience endpoint equivalent to GET .../commands?sort=itime&sort_dir=desc&page_size=1.

docs.title: Global Endpoints
docs.text:
Global command endpoints operate under the path prefix /_api/v3/global_commands and provide read-only cross-workspace access. No {workspace_name} is required.

# Method Path Summary 10 GET /_api/v3/global_commands List commands (global) 11 GET /_api/v3/global_commands/{object_id} Get command (global)

docs.title: 10. List Commands (Global)
docs.text:
Retrieve a paginated list of commands across all accessible workspaces.

Method GET Path /_api/v3/global_commands Auth Required

Query Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description page integer 0 Page number (zero-indexed) page_size integer 20 Number of results per page sort string -- Field to sort by sort_dir string asc Sort direction (asc or desc)

Response: 200 OK

{
  "count": 250,
  "data": [
    {
      "_id": 15,
      "command": "nmap -sV 192.168.1.0/24",
      "tool": "nmap",
      "workspace": "pentest_q1",
      "itime": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
      "...": "..."
    },
    {
      "_id": 28,
      "command": "nessus_scan --target 10.0.0.0/8",
      "tool": "nessus",
      "workspace": "internal_audit",
      "itime": "2026-02-26T14:30:00+00:00",
      "...": "..."
    }
  ]
}

Each command object includes the workspace field to identify which workspace it belongs to.

Example:

curl -s -X GET \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/global_commands?page=0&page_size=50" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -k

Error Codes:

Code Reason 401 Missing or invalid authentication

docs.title: 11. Get Command (Global)
docs.text:
Retrieve a single command by ID without specifying a workspace.

Method GET Path /_api/v3/global_commands/{object_id} Auth Required

Path Parameters:

Parameter Type Description object_id integer Command ID

Response: 200 OK

Returns the full command object including the workspace field.

Example:

curl -s -X GET \
  "https://localhost:5985/_api/v3/global_commands/15" \
  -H "Authorization: Token $TOKEN" \
  -k

Error Codes:

Code Reason 401 Missing or invalid authentication 403 User lacks permission for the command's workspace 404 Command not found

docs.title: Error Responses
docs.text:
All endpoints return errors in the standard Faraday format:

{
  "messages": ["Error description here"]
}
Code Meaning 200 Success (GET, PUT, PATCH) 201 Created (POST) 204 Deleted (DELETE) 400 Bad request -- validation error or malformed JSON 401 Unauthorized -- authentication required or token expired 403 Forbidden -- insufficient workspace permissions 404 Not found -- workspace, command, or endpoint does not exist 500 Internal server error -- check server logs

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• [[README]] -- API endpoint index and common patterns
  • [[activities]] -- Activities API (activity feed with aggregated asset counts)
  • [[bulk-operations]] -- Bulk create, update, and delete operations
  • [[hosts]] -- Hosts API (assets created by commands)
  • [[vulnerabilities]] -- Vulnerabilities API (findings created by commands)

docs.title: Comments API
docs.text:
API Version: v3 Spec Version: Faraday 5.10.0 Base URL: https://localhost:5985 Authentication: Cookie-based session or API token (Authorization: Token <token>) Content-Type: application/json

Comments allow users to attach free-text notes to Faraday objects such as hosts, services, and vulnerabilities. Each comment is scoped to a workspace and linked to a target object via object_type and object_id.

docs.title: Comment Schema
docs.text:
Property Type Required Read-Only Description id integer -- yes Unique identifier (auto-generated) text string yes -- Comment body text object_type string (enum) yes -- Type of the commented object. One of: host, service, comment, vulnerability, project_task comment_type string -- yes Classification of the comment type. object_id integer yes -- ID of the target object receiving the comment creator string -- yes Username of the comment author. create_date datetime -- yes ISO 8601 timestamp of creation. update_date datetime -- yes ISO 8601 timestamp of last update. metadata object -- -- Additional metadata attached to the comment.

Required fields for creation: object_id, object_type, text

object_type enum values:

Value Description host Comment attached to a host service Comment attached to a service vulnerability Comment attached to a vulnerability comment Reply to another comment project_task Comment attached to a project task

docs.title: Example Object
docs.text:
{
  "id": 1,
  "text": "Found during initial pentest reconnaissance",
  "object_type": "vulnerability",
  "comment_type": "",
  "object_id": 42,
  "creator": "faraday",
  "create_date": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
  "update_date": "2026-02-27T10:00:00+00:00",
  "metadata": {}
}

docs.title: Endpoints
docs.text:
All comment endpoints are workspace-scoped under:

/_api/v3/ws/{workspace_name}/comment
# Method Path Summary 1 GET /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment List comments 2 POST /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment Create comment 3 GET /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment/{object_id} Get comment by ID 4 PUT /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment/{object_id} Full update comment 5 PATCH /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment/{object_id} Partial update comment 6 DELETE /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment/{object_id} Delete comment 7 DELETE /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment Bulk delete comments 8 GET /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment/count Count / group comments 9 POST /_api/v3/ws/{ws}/comment_unique Create deduplicated comment

docs.title: 1. List Comments
docs.text:
Retrieve a paginated list of all comments in a workspace.

Request
